slides.title: Notes Inside Your Code
slides.body: A **comment** is a message you leave in your code for humans to read. Python ignores it completely — it's just there to explain what's going on and *why*.
slides.tip: Future-you will thank present-you for every comment you write.

slides.title: Start with a `#` Sign
slides.body: Put `#` anywhere on a line and everything after it becomes a comment. Works on its own line, or tacked on at the end of code.
slides.code:
# This is a full-line comment
print("Hello!")  # This is an inline comment

slides.title: Comment the WHY, Not the WHAT
slides.body: The code already shows *what* happens. A great comment explains **why** you made a choice — the reasoning a future reader can't guess from the code alone.
slides.code:
# BAD: just repeats the code
x = x + 1  # add 1 to x

# GOOD: explains the reason
x = x + 1  # skip index 0 — it's always a placeholder
slides.tip: If the comment just restates the line in English, delete it.

slides.title: Disable Code Temporarily
slides.body: Add `#` to the front of any line to "comment it out" — Python skips it. Handy when testing, without having to delete anything.
slides.code:
name = "Alice"
print("Hello, " + name)
# print("Goodbye, " + name)  # disabled for now

slides.title: Docstrings Document Functions
slides.body: Triple quotes `"""like this"""` right inside a function create a **docstring** — the standard way to describe what a function does. Even `help()` can display it.
slides.tip: Use `#` for quick notes anywhere; use `"""docstrings"""` to describe functions.

slides.title: You're a Commenting Pro!
slides.body: Comments cost nothing and save hours. Write them for the **why**, keep them short, and use docstrings for functions — your future self (and teammates) will be grateful.
slides.tip: Clean up old commented-out code once you're sure you don't need it anymore.
